title: Red Beans and Rice
subtitle: New Orleans Monday staple — dried kidney beans slow-cooked with andouille and the holy trinity
description: Red beans and rice is the Monday institution of New Orleans: dried red kidney beans slow-simmered with the holy trinity, andouille sausage, and a ham hock until some of the beans collapse into the broth and the rest remain whole, creating a thick, creamy, deeply flavoured pot. Served over white rice, it is one of the most satisfying and economical dishes in the American repertoire. The overnight soak is essential; the long cook is where the flavour builds.
sourceNotes: Red beans and rice is a Monday institution in New Orleans, traditionally made on wash day when the laundry occupied the hands and the beans could simmer unattended. The dish is central to New Orleans Creole food culture and appears in The Picayune's Creole Cook Book (1901) and Louis Armstrong, who allegedly signed letters with 'Red beans and ricely yours'.

recipeTools.notes: A large pot with a lid — the beans need plenty of space to cook.

glossaryTerms.definition: The aromatic base of Cajun and Creole cooking: onion, celery, and green bell pepper.

ingredients.notes: Soaked overnight in cold water — drain and rinse before cooking
Sliced or crumbled — or smoked sausage
Optional but traditional — adds body and smoke
Added after 1 hour — salt added too early toughens the bean skins
To taste — served at the table in Louisiana

steps: Drain the soaked beans and rinse well.

Heat the oil in a large casserole over a medium-high heat. Brown the sausage slices for 3–4 minutes. Remove. Add the onion, celery, and green pepper to the fat and cook for 6 minutes until softened. Add the garlic, thyme, smoked paprika, and cayenne for 1 minute.
Return the sausage. Add the drained beans, the ham hock (if using), bay leaves, and enough water or stock to cover by 5 cm. Bring to a vigorous boil and boil uncovered for 10 minutes.
Reduce to a gentle simmer. Cook uncovered, stirring occasionally, for 1 hour. Do not add salt yet.
After 1 hour, add salt and black pepper. Continue to simmer for 45–60 minutes more until the beans are completely tender.
To thicken: remove 2–3 large spoonfuls of beans and mash roughly with a fork or the back of a spoon. Stir the mashed beans back in — they will thicken and cream the broth.
Remove the ham hock, pull any meat from the bone, and return the meat to the pot. Remove the bay leaves. Taste and adjust seasoning.
Serve over cooked white rice, scattered with spring onions and with hot sauce alongside.

content.text: Soak the beans overnight — it reduces the cooking time significantly and helps them cook more evenly. Add the salt only after an hour of cooking; salt added at the start can prevent the bean skins from softening properly. The
of onion, celery, and green pepper is the flavour base: cook it properly before anything else goes in.

Heat the oil and brown {{andouille-sausage}} of sausage. Remove. Cook the trinity (onion, celery, green pepper) in the fat for 6 minutes, add garlic and spices for 1 minute. Return the sausage, add the drained {{dried-kidney-beans}} of beans, ham hock, bay leaves, and {{water}} of water. Boil vigorously for 10 minutes, then reduce to a gentle simmer for 1 hour.

After 1 hour, add salt and pepper. Simmer for 45–60 minutes more until the beans are tender. Mash 2–3 spoonfuls of beans and stir back in to thicken. Remove the ham hock, pull the meat, and return it. Remove the bay leaves. Serve over {{long-grain-rice}} of cooked white rice with spring onions and hot sauce.
